musicalLanguage.description: 'The Water Is Wide' (also known through the related 'O Waly, Waly' and 'The Bonnie Boat' family of ballads) is one of the oldest and most-covered English/Scottish folk songs, its central image — a body of water too wide to cross back over — serving as a metaphor for love and separation across centuries of singers. This arrangement, in G major (the C major label it originally carried had no window support, and the key field has been corrected — see structure), runs a substantial 105 measures at a walking 100 BPM in 2/4, and the note data reveals an accompaniment built almost entirely on a single recurring figure: one repeat group alone (matches at measures 10, 14, 18, 34, 42, 62, 66, 70, 86, 90, and 98) accounts for eleven separate measures sharing the same underlying content — clear evidence of a continuously repeating strophic accompaniment vamp carrying the piece.
musicalLanguage.structure: A strophic ballad built over a single repeating accompaniment cell. Chord-window analysis shows this cell is G-rooted: G major accounts for 72 of the 98 labeled chord windows (73%) and D/D7 another 22 (D7 12%, D 10%), with no C or F chord detected anywhere in the piece — a G-D(7) (I-V) vamp, not the C-F-G (I-IV-V) cadence the C major label this transcription originally carried would predict (the key field has been corrected to G major accordingly). The eleven-measure group (10, 14, 18, 34, 42, 62, 66, 70, 86, 90, 98) and an even larger near-identical group of thirteen measures sharing the same figure show this G-rooted pattern recurring roughly every 4 measures within each verse and then again across every subsequent verse. Density stays gentle and even throughout — among the busiest measures are 7, 23, 31, 47, and 55 (all 11 onsets), spread evenly across the form rather than clustering at a climax, matching the song's plain, walking-tempo accompaniment.
keyMoments: Measure 10, measure 14, measure 18, and measure 34 are the start of an eleven-measure repeat group (also including 42, 62, 66, 70, 86, 90, and 98) — eleven of the piece's 105 measures sharing identical content, direct evidence of one recurring accompaniment figure carrying nearly the whole piece. Chord-window analysis across the piece reads this harmonic world as G major-centered (72 of 98 labeled chord windows, 73%) — the basis for correcting the C major label this transcription originally carried.

That same figure appears in near-identical form across thirteen measures total, including measure 38 and measure 46 alongside the eleven exact matches — meaning across 105 measures, this one accompaniment cell is doing the majority of the harmonic work.

Among the busiest measures, 7, 23, 31, 47, and 55 (all 11 onsets) are spaced roughly every 8 measures early in the piece — a gentle, regular pulse of slightly increased activity rather than one dramatic peak.
The right hand's range stays comfortably singable throughout (C4 to G5, just over an octave), appropriate to a ballad meant to be carried by a solo voice.
Measure 1 opens at just 1 onset, and the left hand doesn't sound until measure 2 — a brief unaccompanied pickup before the recurring G-rooted accompaniment figure establishes itself.
teachingGoals: Repeating accompaniment vamp technique: practice the G-rooted figure that recurs at measures 10, 14, 18, 34, 42, 62, 66, 70, 86, 90, and 98 until it's completely automatic — chord-window analysis shows G major and D/D7 (a I-V vamp, not I-IV-V) covering the great majority of this 105-measure piece, with no C or F chord detected anywhere.
Long-form strophic awareness: learn to recognize where the accompaniment cell restarts for each new verse so you can keep your place across a piece this long without losing count.
Even, walking-tempo phrasing: with density spread gently and evenly (no measure exceeding 11 onsets), practice a steady, unhurried 2/4 pulse rather than reaching for dynamic drama.
Solo ballad range: use the right hand's comfortably singable octave-plus range to practice supporting a vocal line without ever overpowering it.
styleTips: Once the recurring G-rooted accompaniment figure is secure, let your attention go entirely to the melody and its long, singing phrases — the accompaniment should feel automatic, not effortful.
Keep the 2/4 tempo walking and unhurried — this is a ballad about longing and distance, not urgency.
Give the one-note pickup at measure 1 real space before the accompaniment enters on measure 2 — a small breath that sets the reflective tone for what follows.
Avoid adding dynamic swells the data doesn't support — with density this evenly distributed, use phrasing and touch, not volume contrast, to carry the expressive weight.
